column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Lanthanum Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/rare-earth/lanthanide/lanthanum-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for Lanthanum oxide removal without substrate damage
Near-IR wavelength for optimal absorption in Lanthanum oxides
Beam spot diameter for precise oxide removal control
Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage
Fluence threshold for effective Lanthanum cleaning
Fluence threshold for Lanthanum oxide ablation
Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient oxide layer removal
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning efficiency
Recommended number of passes for complete oxide removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage

Typical range for pulsed Nd:YAG lasers in metal cleaning to avoid thermal damage; recommended starting at 100 W for Lanthanum's low melting point (921°C) and moderate thermal conductivity (~13 W/mK). Conservative max to prevent substrate melting. Source: General manufacturer specs (e.g., IPG Photonics) and ablation studies on rare earth metals (e.g., J. Appl. Phys. 2018 on similar metals).

Range for effective contaminant removal without excessive heat buildup; slower for heavy contamination (100-500 mm/s), faster for light (1000-2000 mm/s). Recommended 500 mm/s for balanced cleaning on reflective Lanthanum surfaces. Source: Industry standards from laser cleaning equipment manuals (e.g., Laserax systems) and process optimization in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2020).

Nanosecond regime preferred for industrial cleaning to balance ablation and minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but costlier. 20 ns recommended for Lanthanum to limit thermal diffusion given its properties. Source: Laser physics research in Appl. Surf. Sci. (2019) on metal ablation thresholds.

Typical for pulsed cleaning; higher rates increase speed but risk heat accumulation on low-conductivity Lanthanum. 30 kHz balances efficiency and safety. Trade-off: >50 kHz may cause overheating. Source: Academic literature, e.g., J. Laser Appl. (2021) on repetition rate effects in metal surface prep.

Fluence range for contaminant removal without damaging Lanthanum (estimated damage threshold ~4 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to high reflectivity). Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for safe cleaning of oxides or contaminants. Source: Ablation threshold studies on rare earths (e.g., Phys. Rev. B 2017) and general metal cleaning in Lasers Manuf. Mater. Process. (2022).

Smaller for fine detail (100-200 μm), larger for broad areas (300-500 μm). 200 μm recommended for uniform cleaning on Lanthanum without excessive energy concentration. Source: Equipment specifications from manufacturers like Coherent and Trumpf for 1064 nm systems.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy; 2 passes recommended for complete removal on Lanthanum while minimizing risk to soft substrate. Source: Industry case studies in laser cleaning of metals (e.g., Proc. SPIE 2019) and practical guidelines from Adapt Laser Systems.

Ensures uniform coverage; 50% recommended for Lanthanum to avoid gaps or over-ablation on irregular surfaces. Lower (30%) for speed, higher (80%) for thoroughness. Source: Process optimization studies in J. Clean. Prod. (2021) on scan strategies for metallic substrates.
